title: 12 1 Heat Equation
body:
TOPIC 12 : PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS 2
                12.1 THE HEAT EQUATION


The equation
                                         ∂u       ∂ 2u
                                             = c2 2
                                          ∂t      ∂x
is called the one-dimensional heat equation. It governs the heat flow across a homogenous bar
where c is determined by the thermal properties of the bar.


The boundary conditions

                           u(0, t) = 0,      u(L, t) = 0          for all time t

specify that the bar has length L and that the temperature is maintained at zero on the ends
of the bar for all time. Eventually all the heat will be drawn out of the bar.


Initial conditions take the form:

                      initial temperature distribution                u(x, 0) = f (x)


Solutions (eigenfunctions) are obtained via separation of variables and take the form
                                             nπx −λ2n t
                        un (x, t) = Bn sin      e              for n = 1, 2, 3, · · ·
                                              L
where the λn (eigenvalues) are given by λn = cnπ/L.


The general solution is the superposition of all the eigenfunctions and takes the form
                                                ∞
                                                X              nπx −λ2n t
                                    u(x, t) =         Bn sin      e
                                                n=1
                                                                L



The initial temperature distribution is used to calculate the Bn 0 s and will require the use of
Fourier series and half range expansions when the initial distribution is non-sinusoidal.




                                                 1
 Example 1 Solve the heat equation

                                        ∂u   ∂ 2u
                                           =4 2
                                        ∂t   ∂x
where

u(0, t) = u(10, t) = 0    for all t   (maintained at zero degrees on the ends)

With initial temperature distribution given by
                 
                  x,        0 ≤ x < 5;
u(x, 0) = h(x) =
                    10 − x, 5 ≤ x ≤ 10.
                 




i) By assuming a solution of the form u(x, t) = F (x)G(t) show that

                                       F 00 − kF = 0

and
                                      G0 − 4kG = 0
for k constant. (Note that the D.E. for G is first order!)




                                             2
 ii) By implementing the boundary condition u(0, t) = u(10, t) = 0 show that

                                  F (0) = F (10) = 0




                                          3
 iii) By solving for F with k = 0 and k > 0 show that non-trivial solutions will only arise
from k < 0. (We will say that k = −ρ2 ).




                                            4
 iv) By implementing F (0) = F (10) = 0 with k = −ρ2 show that
                                                  nπx − n2 π2 t
                            un (x, t) = Bn sin(       )e 25
                                                   10




                                           5
 v) Discuss the behaviour of the un as t → ∞




vi)Assuming a final solution of the form
                                   ∞            ∞
                                   X            X               nπx − n2 π2 t
                       u(x, t) =         un =         Bn sin(       )e 25
                                   n=1          n=1
                                                                 10

express Bn in integral form.




                                                 6
                                                              1 R 10           nπx
                                                  F Bn =        0
                                                                    h(x) sin(     )dx F
                                                             5                 10
Lets take a look at the evolution of the temperature for different initial temperature dis-
tributions over a bar of length π:

http://www.math.cornell.edu/∼bterrell/h1/heat1.html

Try a lower resolution and:

u(x,0)=pi/2-abs(x-pi/2)       Left=0,Right=0

u(x,0)=heavi(x-1)-heavi(x-2)      Left=0,Right=0

u(x,0)=pi/2-abs(x-pi/2)        Neumann Left, Neumann Right         (i.e. insulated ends).
